FUMBBL match 4719385 · Sunny Coast Slam practice · 2026-07-18

Mahaha 0–2 Slam Union

End Mahaha conceded, H2 T3

Slam UnionElven Union · Dimitri

The matchup and each side's plan

The Sunny Coast Slam roster rules gave Mahaha 1.10M and seven chosen skills, against Slam Union's 1.12M and eight. Both brought two team rerolls and an apothecary. The larger values shown by FUMBBL were not a useful comparison because the two team listings accounted for added skills differently.

Mahaha entered as the more physical of the two elf teams, though not an attrition team in the usual Blood Bowl sense. AV9 on the Blitzers and linemen supported contact, while Block on both Witch Elves, Dodge on both Blitzers and one Assassin, a Leader Runner and a Wrestle lineman gave Jawa mobile defensive tools. Jawa chose to kick rather than receive. That gave Slam Union the first blocks and the first chance to control the clock, so it surrendered the usual advantage of a bash-oriented opening. In return, Jawa could attack an elf offence with his full roster, look for a defensive score, or force an early Union touchdown and leave time for a reply before receiving after halftime. It was an assertive choice: he backed Mahaha's defence to disrupt the ball or the clock before Union's opening drive could settle.

Slam Union came in with more movement and reliability: Dodge on both Catchers and both Blitzers, Diving Tackle on one Blitzer, a Leader Thrower, a Wrestle lineman and a twelfth player. Frenzy on the other Blitzer added sideline pressure, but also demanded care with follow-ups. Turn 1 showed the cost of getting that positioning wrong; the same Blitzer later decided the match.

Match overview

Very Sunny arrived on the opening kickoff and stayed all match. Dimitri attempted no passes, but the first drive still required three hand-offs. Those rolls ignored the weather modifier, though three extra ball-handling rolls were more than an offence would normally want.

Dimitri used all eight turns for the opening score, but it was not a comfortable stall. Mahaha repeatedly reached the carrier and made the drive genuinely dangerous from turns 3 through 6. Their five stabs produced only two stuns, their carrier blocks found pushes, and Slam Union escaped the pressure without a lasting removal. Union also finished ahead on block volume and knockdowns, but Jawa's defence created chances even though the payoff dice never arrived.

Drive 1 · H1 · Slam Union receiving

T1 · Slam Union

lackofcheese (coach, Slam Union)
My plan was to knock down 2-3 Dark Elves and leave Diving Tackle sitting on them for next turn. I ended up breaking armour anyways, but messed up the positioning - Frenzy blitzer finished in base-to-base contact, giving up an easy 2d on one of my star players. Not good!

Took a shot to rescue it with my lino blitz, but messed that up too. A shambles of a turn 1 :(

The opening produced three armour breaks, but the follow-up positioning exposed the Frenzy Blitzer to an easy two-dice reply. Dimitri tried to rescue him with a lineman blitz; the 3+ dodge failed with three rerolls still banked.

T1 · Mahaha

The reply almost removed Dimitri's Frenzy Blitzer. The KO result forced an immediate apothecary, which left him stunned on the pitch, while both Witch Elves threatened the Union backfield. The early apothecary was aggressive and completely vindicated: the Blitzer played the rest of the match, delivered the game-winning blitz and took an MVP. A Witch Elf also finished behind the Union front line, giving Dimitri bigger things to worry about than continuing to bash the Dark Elf line.

T2 · Slam Union

Slam Union answered by forming a textbook cage around the Leader Thrower at A9/E3, all four corners manned, and punching back along the line. Quiet and correct.

T2 · Mahaha

Correct decline

The Dark Elf Wrestle lineman stunned Slam Union's Diving Tackle Blitzer, useful pressure in its own right. A 5+ carrier blitz existed, but it needed three players and was too likely to end Mahaha's turn. It was too early in the half to justify. A lineman then failed a last-action 2+ dodge, costing nothing more.

T3 · Slam Union

The cage relocated toward the more open west flank at A9/W5, rejoining the Union players already on that side and giving the drive room to advance. The direction was reasonable; relying on two rushes for the final corner was the weak point. At least they came as the final action with the carrier otherwise covered. The Union Wrestle lineman then double-1'd the first rush through the Leader reroll, an emphatic refusal to become that corner.

T3 · Mahaha

Jawa read the hole and chose the right idea. The stronger version used the Dark Elf Wrestle lineman. After one assist-cancellation move, he could have taken a 3+ dodge, a 2+ dodge and two rushes into the one-die blitz. With three rerolls available, that route offered 60.9% ball down without a turnover against 25.3% turnover, because Wrestle makes both-down a clean sack.

Jawa instead used an ordinary lineman on the easier approach. That route could still spill the ball on a both-down, but only by ending the turn. Push, Leader reroll, push again was an unlucky return. The prone Union Wrestle lineman occupied the only legal assist square, so one die was the ceiling for both versions.

T4 · Slam Union

The dicey turn, by Dimitri's own admission. It opened with one of the match's biggest hits: the Diving Tackle Blitzer badly hurt a Block Witch Elf, forcing Jawa to use his apothecary to return her to reserves. The Catcher's next block rolled push and both-down, so Dimitri spent the team reroll and found the knockdown. Three more knockdowns cleared the swarm from the carrier before the Thrower escaped on two rushes to A2/W1. Clearing that contact first was obviously correct. The calmer ball finish was an immediate hand-off to the adjacent Catcher: a 2+ catch with Catch behind it, then MA8 and Dodge to make the same journey without rushing. The trade was one fewer hit on Mahaha. With the team reroll already gone, the Thrower's final two rushes made the turn much scarier.

T4 · Mahaha

Best chance missed

Jawa faced an isolated carrier, and the pursuit was better than it first appeared. Dodge Blitzer #6 could have jumped the prone body on 4+, made 3+ and 2+ dodges, then rushed for the unsupported one-die block. With both rerolls available, the route offered 46.2% ball down without a turnover against 42.6% turnover. That was worth attempting. The Leader Runner and prone Assassin still gave Mahaha a credible chase if the ball came free. Jawa instead played stabs and midfield blocks, banking two more stuns but surrendering the best tempo chance of the drive.

T5 · Slam Union

The Thrower delivered to a Catcher, who settled at H12/W1 behind three bodies near the west sideline. The screen denied every two-dice setup on the board, but that position had a second edge: one square from the sideline, any connected blitz from a Frenzy piece threatened the chain-push into the crowd, ball and all. Safe from dice, not safe from surfing.

T5 · Mahaha

Jawa correctly attempted the surf. Two cancellation moves opened the Witch Elf's lane, after which she needed a 5+ entry and a rush to start the Frenzy chain. With two rerolls, the full route offered 50.5% to dislodge the ball without a turnover, so it was worth taking all-in. The opening dodge failed twice through Dodge and ended the turn before the surf developed.

T6 · Slam Union

The loosest moment of the drive. With the Dark Elves flooding the west side of the pitch, the Catcher relocated to H12/E6 behind a single screener. A second Catcher attempted to dodge away and complete the cover, but rolled double 1 through Dodge. That failure, rather than the intended shape, left the carrier exposed for the reply.

T6 · Mahaha

Jawa found his way in this time, but from the expensive side: the Witch Elf made the 5+ into H12/C for a one-die block. Skull, team reroll, push, push, and the Frenzy chain shoved the Catcher back toward midfield. The eastern route was much better. The prone Dark Elf Wrestle lineman could first stand at A10/C to cancel the defensive assist, Dodge Blitzer #6 could reach H11/E5 as the offensive assist, and Dodge Blitzer #5 could take the two-dice blitz from A12/E5. The moving routes used only 2+ dodges and rushes. With both rerolls committed, the full three-mover setup offered 62.1% ball down without a turnover against 21.9% turnover; holding one still gave 52.2%. This was the clearest first-half attempt, and it should have been taken all-in. Five one-die rolls at the carrier across the half produced only pushes, and this one did not need to be one die.

T7 · Slam Union

Order restored: the Catcher slid to H9/W2 and the box reformed around him, four men, two steps from the line.

T7 · Mahaha

With the ball sealed, there was still one last-turn shot worth taking. Dodge Blitzer #6 had a Diving Tackle-free one-die route at 29.3% ball down without a turnover and 52.2% turnover with the final reroll committed. With Slam Union scoring next and rerolls refreshing, there was no value in saving anything. Mahaha instead blitzed the Thrower and then booted him. The foul stunned him, referee looking elsewhere, but the target only mattered if the injury stuck and a sending-off would have started the second half 10v11. Low upside, real downside. Marginal.

T8 · Slam Union

Hand-off from one Catcher to the other, who rolled a 1 on the 2+ catch and was saved by the Catch reroll. The walk-in made it 1-0 as the half expired.

Drive 2 · H1 · Mahaha receiving

T8 · Mahaha

Two stabs (one stun) and a double-skull block to close the half. Mahaha finished it with zero rerolls and zero removals that stuck.

Drive 3 · H2 · Mahaha receiving

Kickoff

Charge! gave five open Slam Union players a move before Mahaha's drive began. It was a major kickoff result even without a turnover: Union crossed the line, pinned one Assassin and forced Jawa to solve contact before forming an offence. The free blitz on that Assassin was only a push, but the early pressure shaped the next three turns.

T1 · Mahaha

Mahaha produced their best turn of the game: three armour breaks and a clean pickup by the Runner. It still ended when an Assassin rolled the required 2+ dodge and the Diving Tackle Blitzer turned that success into a fall and a stun. That was the skill's second big dividend in consecutive drives, and the Blitzer's position kept steering Dark Elf movement for the next two turns.

T1 · Slam Union

The Frenzy Blitzer lined up a crowd-surf on a Dark Elf lineman near the west sideline; the dice skipped the ceremony. Both-down, Block against no Block, and the lineman was KO'd on the spot. The surf arrived by other means, and it was the only removal of the match that stuck.

T2 · Mahaha

Jawa built a full cage around the Runner at H8/W3, all four corners manned, but needed a 2+ rush to finish the last corner. The shape was secure once built, though a looser screen could have avoided that extra roll. Advancing nothing else was understandable with Union already camped around the drive.

T2 · Slam Union

The Union answer to a full cage was the right one: do not touch it, ring it. A one-die Frenzy blitz was a real option, but it would have begun with a 5+ cage entry, needed both Catchers as cancellation pieces and still risked Dump-Off before the hit. Keeping the Catchers available and forcing the cage to move was better. By the end of the turn the screen was complete, a nine-man net one and two squares off the box, with the Diving Tackle Blitzer controlling both prone Assassins on the east side of the pitch.

T3 · Mahaha

lackofcheese (coach, Slam Union)
From Jawa's point of view he's down a player and in trouble, with Diving Tackle sitting on his two Assassins. Blitzing that sideline lino to break through and take a deeper position was worth it. Maybe could've been sequenced better to leave the ball less exposed on a snake, though?

The pivotal turn. Mahaha were a player down after the KO, both Assassins were controlled by Diving Tackle, and the screen would only tighten. Sitting in the cage meant slow strangulation; breaking toward open ground on the west gave the Runner a genuine scoring chance and was probably worth the risk. The break-out itself was not the error.

The opening Witch Elf blitz was defensible but volatile: Frenzy turned a one-die block into a defender-chosen two-dice block. A failure there would still have left the Runner inside a three-corner pocket, so the ball was reasonably safe. The larger mistake came next. The away-west Blitzer crossed the screen and vacated one remaining corner before the Runner or a replacement screen moved; then the home-west Witch Elf left the other western corner and attempted a rush. She rolled 1, Leader reroll, 1, and broke her own armour. The turn ended with the Runner still at H8/W3, only the Wrestle lineman beside him and both western corners empty.

There was no roll-free deeper cage available after the blitz. The Union screen marked the useful eastern squares, so moving the Runner first would not by itself have made him safe. The lower-risk sequence was to preserve the three-corner pocket while building the break-out: stand the prone lineman, keep the away-west Blitzer beside the Runner, and send at most the home-west Witch Elf forward, stopping before the rush. That would have left the Runner shallow for one more turn, but made a snake on the screen move an inconvenience rather than an immediate clean lane to the ball. If Jawa wanted the deeper position at once, some risk was unavoidable; dismantling both corners before the carrier moved made the worst result unnecessarily severe.

T3 · Slam Union

Turning point

The match-winning sequence came in two acts. First Dimitri cleared the Witch Elf and moved five players to open the recovery and scoring corridor.

On that prepared board, immediately before the blitz, the Frenzy Blitzer could dodge past the Dark Elf Wrestle lineman on two 2+ rolls covered by Dodge and reach the first block without rushing. With two rerolls left, the exact route was 93.1% to put the ball down without a turnover and 6.4% to turn over; holding one reroll still gave 91.7%. A standing first push would require a 2+ rush for the second Frenzy block. Moving the pickup Catcher in as an assist raised the hit slightly, but spending that Catcher for sack chance was the wrong trade when the aim was to recover and score. Preserving both Catchers was a coherent recovery-first choice. The Runner declared Dump-Off into three tacklezones under Very Sunny, a 7+ on a d6, which in Blood Bowl means "natural 6 or bust". With no Block on the Runner, the ball was likely to spill anyway, so taking the one-in-six chance was worth it. The 6 didn't come, and the ball scattered loose before the both-down flattened him.

Then the counter: one Catcher picked up on a 6, rushed and handed off to the other, who made the Nerves of Steel catch, dodged, rushed and scored. Five consecutive 2+ rolls turned a defensive stand into a score in one turn. It was bold and correct with two rerolls still behind it. At 2-0, Jawa pulled the plug with five turns still to play; in a Sunny Coast Slam warm-up, the concession at least cost nothing that mattered. Jawa has since settled on Slann for the tournament, news Dimitri finds troubling: now he has to face the frogs for real.
